loadingTips: Did you know? Zero is both even and not positive!
A rainbow is always round — because it's part of a circle!
The word 'hundred' comes from the Old Norse 'hundrath', meaning 120, not 100!
A clock's minute hand makes a full circle 1,440 times in a year!
The sum of all numbers on a dice opposite faces always equals 7!
A shape with 100 sides is called a hectogon!
The number 9 has a magic trick: multiply any number by 9, the digits always add up to 9!
A honeycomb cell is a perfect hexagon — the strongest shape for its size!
The biggest number with a name is googolplex — 10 to the power of a googol!
Mathematics is the only science where you can always be 100% sure of the answer!
webglNotSupported: Your browser does not support 3D graphics. Try a more modern browser or device.

multiplicationArray.title: Multiplication Arrays
multiplicationArray.description: Build a cube array to solve a multiplication fact
multiplicationArray.instructions: Drag to build, or use the − / + buttons to add rows and columns, then press Check
multiplicationArray.prompt: Build an array of {rows} rows × {cols} columns

seo.intro: Multiplication Arrays has students construct a 3-D cube grid by choosing the number of rows and columns, then pressing Check to confirm the product. Unlike the existing Multiplication Array game that presents a pre-built fruit grid, here students actively build the rectangle themselves — making the equation and its visual proof one action.
howToPlay: Read the target multiplication fact shown (e.g. 3 rows × 4 columns).
Drag or use −/+ buttons to set the number of rows and columns.
Watch the cube grid grow to fill the rectangle you have specified.
Press Check to confirm that rows × columns equals the stated product.
seo.skills: Building the array trains students to see multiplication as a rectangular area — rows times columns — rather than a sequence of additions. Choosing both dimensions themselves reinforces that either factor can be rows or columns, concretising the commutative property before formal proof.
seo.example: For 4×6 the student sets 4 rows and 6 columns, filling a 24-cube rectangle. Rotating the view to see 6 rows of 4 makes 4×6=6×4 immediately obvious without any teacher explanation.
seo.mistakes: Students sometimes confuse rows and columns, placing 6 rows and 4 columns for a 4×6 prompt. The game accepts either orientation — the product is the same — helping them discover commutativity rather than treating the order as a rule to memorise.

faqs.q: How does building the array differ from the Multiplication Array (fruit grid) game?
faqs.a: The fruit grid presents a ready-made array and asks for the count; this game gives the factors and asks students to construct the rectangle themselves, making the connection from equation to shape active rather than observational.

faqs.q: Which grades is Multiplication Arrays for?
faqs.a: Grades 3–4, where students move from repeated addition to the area model and need to internalise both factors of a product before formal times-table fluency.

faqs.q: Does the game cover all times tables?
faqs.a: It covers the multiplication facts students encounter in grades 3–4, with arrays that can grow large enough to represent products up to 100.

measureFill.title: Fill the Container
measureFill.description: Fill the container to the target volume
measureFill.instructions: Drag to pour liquid to the target mark
measureFill.wrong: Almost! Get closer to the target mark

seo.intro: Fill the Container turns reading a volume into a hands-on pouring task. Students drag to fill a jar to a target millilitre mark, learning that capacity is measured continuously along a scale rather than as a whole-number count. The live liquid level gives instant feedback on estimation accuracy.
howToPlay: Read the target volume shown above the container.
Drag upward to pour liquid in, watching the level rise against the marked scale.
Slow down as you approach the target mark — the last few millilitres matter.
Press Check when the liquid level lines up with the target graduation.
seo.skills: Practising volume measurement builds the understanding that capacity is a continuous quantity read from a scale, not a count of objects. Students learn to read intermediate graduations and develop the precision needed before moving to multi-step capacity problems in grades 3–4.
seo.example: The target is 350 ml. The jar's scale shows marks every 50 ml. Students pour to the 300 mark, then carefully add the remaining 50 ml to reach 350 — learning to use major markings as stepping stones to an exact reading.
seo.mistakes: Students often overshoot the target and stop only when the level clearly passes the mark. The game encourages slower, more deliberate pouring near the target, building the habit of approaching a measured value from below rather than correcting an overfill.

faqs.q: How does dragging to fill the container teach volume differently from writing an answer?
faqs.a: Pouring links the abstract number to a physical level — students see the relationship between the digit and the height of liquid, making millilitres feel real rather than arbitrary.

faqs.q: Which grades is Fill the Container designed for?
faqs.a: Grades 3–4, where the curriculum introduces standard capacity units and reading graduated scales in everyday contexts.

faqs.q: How is this different from the Ruler Measure game?
faqs.a: Fill the Container measures volume (ml) by reading a vertical liquid scale; Ruler Measure measures length (cm) by positioning two flags on a horizontal ruler. Both practise scale-reading but for different measurement attributes.

fractionBuild.title: Fraction Builder
fractionBuild.description: Fill parts of the pie to match the fraction
fractionBuild.instructions: Tap slices to fill them, then press Check

seo.intro: Fraction Builder turns an abstract fraction into a pie you fill with your own hands. You read a target such as 3/4, then shade slices until the pie matches — so the symbol stops being two numbers and becomes a visible amount.
howToPlay: Read the target fraction shown above the pie.
Tap or drag slices to shade them, counting up to the numerator.
Notice the denominator decides how many equal slices the whole is cut into.
Press Check to confirm the shaded part matches the target fraction.
seo.skills: It builds the core idea that the denominator names the size of each equal part while the numerator counts how many you take — the foundation students need before comparing or adding fractions.
seo.example: For 3/4 the pie is cut into 4 equal slices and you shade 3. Seeing the single empty slice makes it obvious that 3/4 is one slice short of a whole, not just 'three and four'.
seo.mistakes: Many students read 3/4 as 'three of four separate things' and miss that the parts must be equal. Building the pie forces equal slices and corrects the most common early fraction error.

faqs.q: Which grades is Fraction Builder for?
faqs.a: It fits grades 3-4, where students first meet fractions as equal parts of one whole.

faqs.q: Do students need multiplication before this game?
faqs.a: No. Fraction Builder only needs counting and the idea of equal sharing, so it works as a first introduction to fractions.

faqs.q: How is this different from the Fraction Number Line game?
faqs.a: Fraction Builder shows a fraction as part of one whole (a pie); Fraction Number Line places the same fraction as a point between 0 and 1 — two complementary models.

areaPerimeter.title: Area & Perimeter
areaPerimeter.description: Build a rectangle with a given area or perimeter
areaPerimeter.instructions: Drag to resize the garden, or use the width/height − / + buttons, then press Check
areaPerimeter.wrong: Almost! Resize the garden and try again

seo.intro: Area and Perimeter presents a garden you can resize by dragging its edges. Sometimes the challenge asks for a specific area, sometimes a specific perimeter — and the key discovery is that two rectangles with the same area can have completely different perimeters, and vice versa.
howToPlay: Read whether the target is an area (square units) or a perimeter (linear units).
Drag the garden's edge or use the width and height −/+ buttons to resize it.
Watch the live readouts: area (length × width) and perimeter (2 × length + 2 × width) update together.
Press Check when the correct measurement matches the target.
seo.skills: Distinguishing area from perimeter is one of the most persistent confusions in primary geometry. Working with both measurements on the same shape — and seeing them change independently as you resize — builds the conceptual separation that formulas alone cannot.
seo.example: A 3 × 4 rectangle has area 12 and perimeter 14. A 2 × 6 rectangle also has area 12 but perimeter 16. Resizing between them on the same screen makes the contrast immediate and memorable.
seo.mistakes: Most students add all four side lengths for area (confusing it with perimeter) or multiply length and width for perimeter. Watching both numbers change simultaneously as the garden grows corrects both errors at once.

faqs.q: Can the same target area be reached with different rectangles?
faqs.a: Yes. Many area targets have more than one solution (for example area 12 works for 2×6, 3×4, or 1×12). The game accepts any valid rectangle, making the task open-ended by design.

faqs.q: Which grades is this game for?
faqs.a: It suits grades 3-4, when students learn the formulas for area and perimeter of rectangles and need to see them as separate measurements.

faqs.q: How is this different from the Geoboard game, which also measures area?
faqs.a: Area and Perimeter constrains the shape to a rectangle and focuses on comparing area vs. perimeter of the same shape. Geoboard lets students build any polygon and targets area alone — more open, different skill.

tenFrame.title: Ten Frame Fill
tenFrame.description: Fill the ten-frame with marbles up to the target number
tenFrame.instructions: Drag across the holes to fill marbles, or use the − / + buttons, then press Check
tenFrame.wrong: Almost! Count the marbles again and fix it

seo.intro: Ten Frame Fill places students in front of a 2×5 grid of holes and asks them to fill the correct number of marbles. The fixed ten-slot frame anchors every number from 1 to 10 to its visual distance from ten, making the structure of small numbers concrete and automatic.
howToPlay: Read the target number shown above the ten-frame.
Drag across the holes or use −/+ to add or remove marbles.
Notice how many slots remain empty — that many are needed to reach ten.
When the filled slots match the target, press Check.
seo.skills: The ten-frame builds the 'friend of ten' concept: every number from 1–9 is understood as a certain number of marbles plus a certain number of empty holes. This relationship to ten accelerates mental addition (7+5=10+2) and subtraction far beyond what counting-on achieves.
seo.example: Placing 7 marbles fills the top row completely (5) and 2 slots in the bottom row, leaving 3 empty. The student immediately sees 7=5+2 and 10−7=3, without counting.
seo.mistakes: Students who simply count to the target number miss the ten-frame's key lesson: the relationship between the number and ten. Teachers can prompt by asking 'how many are empty?' alongside 'how many are filled?' to activate the complementary thinking the frame is designed for.

faqs.q: How does Ten Frame Fill differ from Number Bond Splitter?
faqs.a: Ten Frame Fill always splits a number relative to ten — filled vs. empty slots in a fixed 10-cell grid. Number Bond Splitter splits any small number into any two arbitrary parts, training flexible decomposition without anchoring to ten.

faqs.q: Which grades is Ten Frame Fill for?
faqs.a: Grades 1–2, where students are building mental images of numbers 1–10 and their relationships to ten as the foundation for addition and subtraction facts.

faqs.q: Can numbers larger than 10 appear in the game?
faqs.a: The standard game covers 1–10 to match the ten-frame structure. Some rounds may use a double ten-frame to show numbers 11–20, helping students see teen numbers as ten plus some ones.

placeValue.title: Place Value Builder
placeValue.description: Build the number from base-ten blocks in the warehouse
placeValue.instructions: Add boxes to each place with the − / + buttons (hundreds, tens, ones), or drag upward over a column to add. When a column reaches 10, ten small boxes bundle into one bigger box. Press Check to grade
placeValue.wrong: Almost! Check the digits and fix the number

seo.intro: Place Value Builder sets students in a warehouse where they stack physical blocks into hundreds, tens, and ones columns to construct a target number. When any column reaches ten blocks, they automatically bundle into one larger unit — making the base-ten trading rule an observable event rather than a memorised procedure.
howToPlay: Read the target number shown above the warehouse columns.
Use −/+ or drag upward on each column to add hundreds, tens, or ones blocks.
Watch ten ones bundle into a ten-rod and ten rods bundle into a hundred-flat automatically.
When all three columns match the digits of the target number, press Check.
seo.skills: Place Value Builder develops the understanding that each digit's position determines its value: a 3 in the hundreds column is worth 300, not 3. Students practise composing multi-digit numbers from their constituent parts and see regrouping as a natural consequence of the base-ten system.
seo.example: To build 234: add 2 hundreds-flats, 3 ten-rods, and 4 ones-cubes. The warehouse shows 200+30+4 stacked separately, then Check confirms the combined value is 234.
seo.mistakes: Students often enter the digit, not the value — placing 2 ones when asked for 234 because the digit '4' is the one they see last. The warehouse's colour-coded columns (blue for hundreds, yellow for tens, red for ones) train them to read each digit in its correct column before placing blocks.

faqs.q: How does Place Value Builder differ from Exploding Dots?
faqs.a: Exploding Dots focuses on the moment of regrouping — ten dots exploding into one in the next column — to make carrying visceral. Place Value Builder focuses on building a static number correctly from its digits; regrouping is a side-effect, not the main event.

faqs.q: Which grades is Place Value Builder for?
faqs.a: Grades 2–4, spanning two-digit numbers in grade 2 through three-digit numbers and early four-digit numbers in grades 3 and 4.

faqs.q: Can the same number be built in more than one way?
faqs.a: No. The target is a specific digit decomposition (e.g. 2 hundreds, 3 tens, 4 ones for 234). Students must match the standard form, training canonical place-value representation.

clockBuilder.title: Clock Time Builder
clockBuilder.description: Set the clock hands to match a digital time
clockBuilder.instructions: Drag the minute hand around the clock (the hour hand follows proportionally), or use the hour ±1 and minute ±5 buttons, then press Check
clockBuilder.correct: Great! You set the clock to {time}
clockBuilder.wrong: Almost! Check the hour and the minutes and fix it

seo.intro: Clock Time Builder bridges digital and analogue time by asking students to drag clock hands to match a displayed digital time. As the minute hand moves, the hour hand follows proportionally — so students see the relationship between the two hands in real time rather than memorising isolated positions.
howToPlay: Read the digital time displayed above the clock face.
Drag the minute hand to the correct minute position, watching the hour hand follow.
Fine-tune the hour and minute with the ±1 and ±5 buttons if needed.
Press Check once both hands point to the right positions.
seo.skills: Setting analogue hands to match a digital time builds two skills simultaneously: reading the minute scale in 5-minute steps and understanding that the hour hand moves continuously across the dial rather than jumping from number to number. Both skills are required for telling time fluently in grades 2–3.
seo.example: For 3:45, the minute hand points to the 9 (45 minutes) and the hour hand sits three-quarters of the way between 3 and 4. Moving the minute hand to 45 makes the hour hand shift visibly — showing why the hour reading changes as minutes advance.
seo.mistakes: Students often set the hour hand to the exact hour number even when the time is past the half-hour. Seeing the hour hand land between two numbers (not on one) corrects the assumption that analogue and digital hours always align at the same position.

faqs.q: What does dragging the minute hand show that a static clock diagram does not?
faqs.a: The live proportional movement of the hour hand demonstrates that both hands move continuously and are linked — a key insight students miss when they only practise reading static images.

faqs.q: Which grades is Clock Time Builder designed for?
faqs.a: Grades 2–3, where the curriculum covers telling time to the nearest 5 minutes and reading both analogue and digital formats.

faqs.q: How is this different from the Ruler Measure or Weight Balance games?
faqs.a: Clock Time Builder measures time using a circular dial with two moving hands; the other measurement games use linear scales or pan balances. All four games teach reading a standard measurement tool, but each targets a different attribute and unit.

fractionNumberLine.title: Fraction Number Line
fractionNumberLine.description: Place a fraction on the number line between 0 and 1
fractionNumberLine.instructions: Drag the bead along the tape to the target fraction (it snaps to the nearest tick), or use the numerator ±1 and denominator ±1 buttons. The denominator subdivides the line live. Then press Check
fractionNumberLine.wrong: Almost! Drag the bead to the right spot and fix it

seo.intro: Fraction Number Line moves the fraction off the pie and onto a ruler between 0 and 1. By dragging a bead to the exact position, students discover that a fraction is a distance, not just a count of shaded pieces.
howToPlay: Read the target fraction displayed above the number line.
Use the denominator ±1 buttons to subdivide the line into the right number of equal intervals.
Drag the bead to the correct tick, or fine-tune with the numerator ±1 buttons.
Press Check when the bead sits on the target position.
seo.skills: Placing fractions on a number line prepares students for comparing fractions by size, finding equivalent fractions, and eventually understanding fractions greater than 1 — all of which depend on seeing fractions as distances rather than parts of a shape.
seo.example: For 2/3 the line is divided into 3 equal intervals and the bead belongs on the second tick. Changing the denominator to 6 shows immediately that 4/6 lands on the same point, making equivalent fractions visible without any calculation.
seo.mistakes: Students often place the bead at the tick that matches the numerator without accounting for the denominator. The live subdivision makes clear that the denominator controls how large each step is — both numbers are needed together.

faqs.q: What does the number line teach that a pie diagram does not?
faqs.a: The number line shows a fraction as a position between two whole numbers, which is the model students need for ordering, adding, and later understanding decimals.

faqs.q: Which grades is this game for?
faqs.a: It suits grades 3-4, after students have met fractions as parts of a whole and are ready to see them as points on a line.

faqs.q: What happens when I change the denominator mid-game?
faqs.a: The line resubdivides in real time, so the tick marks shift and the bead shows its position relative to the new scale — a live demonstration of how the denominator sets step size.

algebraBalance.title: Algebra Balance
algebraBalance.description: Remove equal crystals from both sides of the scale until the ? stands alone
algebraBalance.instructions: The scale starts balanced. Remove an equal number of crystals from both sides (Left −/+, Right −/+) until the ? stands alone and the beam is level again. Removing unequally tips the heavier side down. Then press Check
algebraBalance.wrong: Almost! Remove an equal number from both sides until the ? stands alone and the scale is balanced

seo.intro: Algebra Balance bridges arithmetic and algebra by making an equation visible. The scale starts balanced with crystals on both sides and an unknown crystal marked with a question mark. Students remove equal amounts from each side until the unknown stands alone — performing the inverse operation physically rather than symbolically.
howToPlay: Look at the balanced scale: one side has the unknown (?) plus some crystals, the other side has only crystals.
Use the Left −/+ and Right −/+ buttons to remove the same number of crystals from both sides.
Keep removing equal amounts until the ? stands alone on one side.
When the beam is level with ? isolated, press Check to confirm the solution.
seo.skills: Algebra Balance teaches the central principle of equation solving: whatever you do to one side, you must do to the other. This hands-on inverse-operation experience prepares students for formal algebra far better than memorizing rules about moving terms across the equals sign.
seo.example: The scale shows ? + 4 = 9. Remove 4 crystals from each side: left becomes ?, right becomes 5. The beam levels and ? = 5. Students feel the logic rather than guessing.
seo.mistakes: Beginners often remove crystals only from one side to clear the unknown. When they do this the beam tips immediately, providing instant corrective feedback without any explanation needed.

faqs.q: What equation type does Algebra Balance practise?
faqs.a: It covers equations of the form ? + b = c, targeting grades 5–6 where students first encounter formal unknowns and inverse operations.

faqs.q: How is this different from Balance Scale?
faqs.a: Balance Scale asks students to match a given total using numbered weights — it practises arithmetic addition. Algebra Balance starts with equality already established and asks students to isolate an unknown by inverse removal — it practises algebraic thinking.

faqs.q: Do students need prior algebra knowledge?
faqs.a: No prior algebra knowledge is required. The concrete scale model lets students discover the equal-removal principle themselves before meeting formal notation.

balanceScale.title: Balance Scale
balanceScale.description: Build the right pan with numbered weights so it equals the left pan
balanceScale.instructions: Read the weights on the cubes in the left pan and add them up. Add numbered weights to the right pan with the plus and minus buttons (1, 2, 5, 10) until the beam is level — when it is level, both sides are equal. Then press Check
balanceScale.wrong: Almost! Add or remove weights on the right until the beam is level

seo.intro: Balance Scale shows what an equation means before students ever write one. The left pan holds mystery cubes; students add numbered weights (1, 2, 5, 10) to the right pan until the beam is exactly level. Because the two sides must be physically equal, the idea that both sides of an equation share the same value becomes concrete and undeniable.
howToPlay: Read the numbered cubes stacked in the left pan and add up their total.
Use the +/− buttons to add or remove weights (1, 2, 5, 10) in the right pan.
Adjust until the beam is perfectly level — both pans hold the same total.
Press Check once the scale is balanced.
seo.skills: Balance Scale builds the foundational understanding of equality — that both sides of an equals sign must hold the same value. Students practice mental addition and decomposition while the visual feedback of a levelling beam makes each correct answer immediately satisfying.
seo.example: The left pan holds cubes worth 3 + 7 + 5 = 15. Students add weights to the right: two 5s and one 10 give 20 — too heavy. Swap one 5 for nothing and add a 1 and a 2 to reach 15. The beam levels.
seo.mistakes: Students often overshoot and keep adding weights when the beam has already tipped past level. Learning to remove weights (decrease) is as important as adding, and the game requires both directions of adjustment.

faqs.q: Which grade range is Balance Scale aimed at?
faqs.a: Grades 2–5, where students practise addition, mental arithmetic, and early equation concepts with increasing number sizes.

faqs.q: How is this different from Algebra Balance?
faqs.a: Balance Scale focuses on arithmetic: students calculate a total and replicate it with weights. Algebra Balance already starts balanced and challenges students to isolate an unknown by removing equal amounts — that is algebraic thinking, not arithmetic matching.

faqs.q: Can the right pan have multiple solutions?
faqs.a: Yes. There are many weight combinations that reach the same total, and the game accepts any correct combination — encouraging students to explore decomposition rather than find one fixed answer.

moneyShop.title: Money Shop
moneyShop.description: Pay the exact amount in shekels at a market stall
moneyShop.instructions: Drag up over the tray to add a ₪1 coin, or use each coin and bill's − / + buttons (₪1, ₪2, ₪5, ₪10, ₪20, ₪50). Add coins and bills until the tray reaches the amount, then press Check
moneyShop.wrong: Almost! Check the amount on the tray and fix it

seo.intro: Money Shop places students inside a market stall where they must pay exact amounts using coins and bills. They drag real-looking currency onto a tray, watching the running total until it matches the price — turning abstract coin values into a lived shopping transaction.
howToPlay: Read the price shown on the shop sign.
Drag coins and bills onto the tray or use the −/+ buttons to add each denomination.
Watch the tray total rise as you add each piece of currency.
When the tray matches the price exactly, press Check to complete the purchase.
seo.skills: Money Shop builds fluency with coin and bill denominations and multi-denomination addition. Students learn to choose the fewest coins needed for a price and to give change by finding the difference between the amount tendered and the price.
seo.example: To pay ₪17, a student might place one ₪10 bill, one ₪5 coin, and two ₪1 coins. They see 10+5+1+1=17 on the tray, confirming the combination before pressing Check.
seo.mistakes: Students often stack too many small coins rather than choosing efficient combinations. The tray total provides immediate feedback, encouraging them to swap for larger denominations when the total overshoots the price.

faqs.q: How does the game teach making change, not just paying exact amounts?
faqs.a: Some rounds show an amount already on the tray and ask the student to adjust it — adding or removing coins to reach the exact price, practising both overpaying and correcting.

faqs.q: Which grades is Money Shop for?
faqs.a: Grades 2–4, covering early coin recognition through multi-step change calculations as the price complexity increases.

faqs.q: Does the game use real Israeli currency denominations?
faqs.a: Yes. The coins and bills are ₪1, ₪2, ₪5, ₪10, ₪20, and ₪50 — the denominations students encounter in daily life in Israel.

additionMine.title: Addition Mine
additionMine.description: Build the sum of the addition from ore crystals in an underground mine
additionMine.instructions: Build the sum using each digit's − / + buttons (hundreds, tens, ones), or drag up over a column to add a crystal. When a column fills past 9, an ore cart rides the rail to carry the group to the next place. Press Check to check
additionMine.wrong: Almost! Check the digits and fix the sum

seo.intro: Addition Mine puts place-value columns right in front of you. Students build a target sum by filling a ones column, tens column, and hundreds column with ore crystals. When a column overflows ten, an ore cart carries the group to the next place — so regrouping becomes visible instead of abstract.
howToPlay: Read the two numbers shown at the top of the mine shaft.
Use the −/+ buttons (or drag up) to add ore crystals to the ones, tens, and hundreds columns.
When a column reaches ten crystals, watch the cart carry them to the next column — that is regrouping.
Keep adding until the total matches the target sum, then press Check.
seo.skills: Addition Mine builds the concrete understanding of regrouping (carrying) that underpins column addition. By physically filling columns and watching ten units bundle into one at the next place, students stop confusing digit positions and start seeing why the algorithm works.
seo.example: Adding 47 + 36: fill 7 ones and 6 ones — the cart carries ten to the tens column, leaving 3 ones. Then add 4 tens and 3 tens plus the carried one to get 8 tens. Result: 83.
seo.mistakes: Students often add all the digits without considering place value, writing 47 + 36 = 713 instead of 83. Watching the cart move ten crystals physically into the next column corrects this column-blindness before it becomes a habit.

faqs.q: Which grades is Addition Mine designed for?
faqs.a: It suits grades 2–4, covering 2-digit and 3-digit addition with regrouping — the core progression in early elementary arithmetic.

faqs.q: Does the game always require regrouping?
faqs.a: No. Some problems have no carrying; others require carrying once or twice. The mine shaft only shows the cart animation when a column actually overflows ten.

faqs.q: How is this different from Balance Scale?
faqs.a: Addition Mine builds a sum step by step using place-value columns and regrouping. Balance Scale requires students to find a total and match it by selecting weights — the emphasis is on equality rather than column arithmetic.

subtractionBridge.title: Subtraction Bridge
subtractionBridge.description: Take the stone bridge apart block by block to reach the difference, breaking a ten-stone when a column runs out
subtractionBridge.instructions: Remove stones with the − / + buttons for each digit (hundreds, tens, ones), or drag down over a column to remove a stone. When a column is too small, press − to break a ten-stone from the next digit into ten little stones (the bridge breaks). Then press Check
subtractionBridge.wrong: Almost! Check the digits and fix the difference

seo.intro: Subtraction Bridge asks students to dismantle a stone bridge block by block, column by column, to find the difference between two numbers. When the ones column has fewer stones than needed, a ten-stone from the next column breaks apart into ten small stones — making the borrowing (regrouping) step a physical demolition rather than a pencil rule.
howToPlay: Read the subtraction problem shown above the bridge.
Remove stones from the ones, tens, or hundreds column using −/+ or by dragging down.
When a column runs out before the subtrahend digit, press − to break a ten-stone from the next column into ten smaller stones.
Once all columns show the correct difference, press Check.
seo.skills: Subtraction Bridge concretises borrowing (regrouping) in multi-digit subtraction: the single most confusing written-algorithm step. Students who see a ten literally break into ten ones understand why the digit in the next column decreases by 1, preventing the most persistent subtraction error in primary school.
seo.example: Subtracting 47 from 83: the ones column has 3 but needs 7. Press − to break a ten-stone: the tens column goes from 8 to 7, and the ones go from 3 to 13. Now 13−7=6 and 7−4=3, giving 36.
seo.mistakes: The classic error is subtracting the smaller digit from the larger regardless of which is on top (e.g. computing 83−47 as 40+4=44). The bridge mechanic forces the student to actually remove the correct quantity, making the direction of subtraction unambiguous.

faqs.q: How does the bridge mechanic teach borrowing without the abstract written rule?
faqs.a: When a column has too few stones, pressing − physically breaks a ten-block into ten ones right in front of the student. They see the tens digit decrease and the ones pool increase — the written borrow rule is a shorthand for that event.

faqs.q: Which grades is Subtraction Bridge for?
faqs.a: Grades 2–4, from two-digit subtraction with borrowing in grade 2 through three-digit problems with double borrowing in grades 3 and 4.

faqs.q: What happens if students need to borrow from the hundreds into the tens?
faqs.a: The game supports cascading borrows: if the tens column also runs out, pressing − breaks a hundred-stone into ten tens, showing a two-step borrow visually.

volumeBuilder.title: Volume Builder
volumeBuilder.description: Build a box of unit cubes that has the given volume
volumeBuilder.instructions: Set the length, width and height with the − / + buttons (or drag: up = longer, right = wider; height uses the buttons). The cubes fill the tank layer by layer. When you are done, press Check
volumeBuilder.wrong: Almost! Change the length, width or height and try again

rulerMeasure.title: Ruler Measure
rulerMeasure.description: Measure the length of the object lying on the ruler, in centimeters
rulerMeasure.instructions: Line up the left flag with the object's left end and the right flag with its right end, using the left ±1 and right ±1 buttons (or drag). Note: the object doesn't always start at 0, so length = right reading − left reading. Then press Check
rulerMeasure.correct: Great! The object is {length} cm long
rulerMeasure.wrong: Almost! Line each flag up with the matching end of the object and fix it

seo.intro: Ruler Measure places an object anywhere along a ruler and asks students to position two flags at its ends. Because the object does not always start at zero, students must subtract the two readings to find the length — practising the real-world skill of measuring from an arbitrary starting point.
howToPlay: Look at the object resting on the ruler and find where its left end sits.
Drag or press the left-flag buttons to align the left flag with the object's left end.
Use the right-flag buttons (or drag) to align the right flag with the object's right end.
Subtract: length = right reading minus left reading, then press Check.
seo.skills: Measuring from a non-zero start teaches that length is a difference between two positions, not a single reading. This understanding is essential for using real rulers, tape measures, and number lines — and prevents the common error of always assuming the object begins at zero.
seo.example: The object's left end sits at 3 cm and its right end at 11 cm. The length is 11 − 3 = 8 cm. Students who only ever measure from zero would read 11 and write the wrong answer; this game forces the subtraction step every time.
seo.mistakes: The most common error is reading only the right flag's position and ignoring the left flag's offset. Placing both flags independently before calculating makes the subtraction unavoidable and builds correct measurement habits for grades 2–4.

faqs.q: Why does the object not always start at zero on the ruler?
faqs.a: Real-world measuring rarely starts at zero — tape measures, broken rulers, and offset objects are common. Practising non-zero starts prevents the habit of relying on a perfect alignment and builds genuine measurement skill.

faqs.q: Which grades is Ruler Measure designed for?
faqs.a: Grades 2–4, covering the progression from simple centimetre counting to reading a ruler at any position and calculating the difference.

faqs.q: How is this different from the Fill the Container game?
faqs.a: Ruler Measure works with length in centimetres using a horizontal ruler and two position flags; Fill the Container works with volume in millilitres using a vertical liquid scale. Both practise reading a graduated scale but for different measurement attributes.

decimalBuilder.title: Decimal Builder
decimalBuilder.description: Build a decimal number by filling sand jars in a lab
decimalBuilder.instructions: Fill the ones, tenths and hundredths jars with the − / + buttons (each jar's sand level shows that digit), then press Check
decimalBuilder.wrong: Almost! Check the ones, tenths and hundredths and fix the number

seo.intro: Decimal Builder puts students in a lab where three sand jars represent ones, tenths, and hundredths. They fill each jar to the correct level to construct a target decimal, turning abstract place-value notation into a visible, physical quantity they can read at a glance.
howToPlay: Read the target decimal shown above the jars.
Use the ones jar's +/- buttons to set the digit before the decimal point.
Use the tenths jar to set the first decimal digit, and the hundredths jar for the second.
The sand level in each jar shows the current digit — adjust until all three match the target.
Press Check to confirm your number.
seo.skills: Decimal Builder develops decimal place value: students practise reading each digit's column (ones, tenths, hundredths) and translating it into a physical quantity. This is the foundational step before comparing, ordering, or adding decimal numbers.
seo.example: For the target 2.35: fill the ones jar to 2, the tenths jar to 3, and the hundredths jar to 5. Seeing three separate jars side by side makes the structure 2 + 0.3 + 0.05 obvious before any arithmetic is attempted.
seo.mistakes: Students often treat decimals as whole numbers, entering 35 into the hundredths jar for 0.35. Separate jars with individual levels force them to work one column at a time, breaking the habit of reading across the decimal point without pausing.

faqs.q: How do the sand jars show decimal place value differently from the block columns in Place Value Builder?
faqs.a: Place Value Builder uses stacking blocks for whole-number hundreds, tens, and ones. Decimal Builder uses sand-fill jars for ones, tenths, and hundredths, making the fractional columns visible without blocks.

faqs.q: Which grades is Decimal Builder for?
faqs.a: Grades 4-5, where students first meet tenths and hundredths as named place-value positions.

faqs.q: Can students set a digit greater than 9 in any jar?
faqs.a: No. Each jar is capped at 9, so the game keeps students within standard decimal notation and avoids the carrying step handled separately in Decimal Addition.

decimalNumberLine.title: Decimal on a Number Line
decimalNumberLine.description: Place a decimal number on a number line — practice ordering and magnitude of decimals
decimalNumberLine.instructions: Drag the train to the correct position on the line (0 to 1), then press Check
decimalNumberLine.correct: Correct! {decimal} is in the right position
decimalNumberLine.wrong: Almost! Check the position and try again

seo.intro: Decimal Number Line asks students to drag a train to the exact position of a decimal between 0 and 1. By placing the number on a ruler rather than in a jar or column, the game teaches students that a decimal is a precise distance along a scale, not just a pattern of digits.
howToPlay: Read the target decimal shown above the number line.
Estimate where the decimal falls between 0 and 1.
Drag the train along the line to the estimated position.
Fine-tune until the train sits at the correct point.
Press Check to confirm.
seo.skills: Placing decimals on a number line trains students to compare decimal size by position, reinforcing that 0.7 is larger than 0.3 because it sits further right. This spatial sense of magnitude is the basis for ordering decimals and linking them to fractions.
seo.example: For 0.6: the line runs from 0 to 1 and the train should land at the sixth tick of ten equal intervals. Comparing it to the fraction 6/10 shows that 0.6 and 6/10 name the same point.
seo.mistakes: Students frequently confuse decimal size with digit count: they place 0.35 to the right of 0.9 because 35 looks larger than 9. The number line's fixed scale exposes that error immediately by showing 0.9 is very close to 1 while 0.35 is barely past one third.

faqs.q: How does placing a decimal on the line differ from placing a fraction in the Fraction Number Line game?
faqs.a: Fraction Number Line uses a subdivided ruler with adjustable tick-mark spacing to place fractions like 2/3. Decimal Number Line uses a fixed decimal scale (tenths) to place values like 0.6, building fluency with the decimal notation specifically.

faqs.q: Which grades is Decimal Number Line for?
faqs.a: Grades 3-5: the core task suits grade 3-4 students meeting tenths, and the hundredths targets extend into grade 5.

faqs.q: Does the game cover numbers greater than 1?
faqs.a: No. The line runs from 0 to 1 to keep focus on the decimal part. Ordering and comparing multi-digit decimals is introduced in later activities once the fractional portion is secure.

decimalAddition.title: Decimal Addition
decimalAddition.description: Add two decimal numbers digit by digit — practice addition with carrying
decimalAddition.instructions: Build the sum: set the ones, tenths and hundredths with the − / + buttons, then press Check
decimalAddition.wrong: Almost! Check each digit separately and remember to carry

seo.intro: Decimal Addition has students build the sum of two decimal numbers column by column using digit wheels for ones, tenths, and hundredths. The game makes the align-then-add procedure concrete: each column is set independently so carrying across the decimal point becomes visible.
howToPlay: Read the two decimal addends shown on screen.
Add the hundredths digits first; use the hundredths wheel to set that digit of the sum.
If the hundredths total is 10 or more, carry 1 to the tenths column.
Repeat for tenths, then ones, carrying as needed.
Press Check when all three wheels show the correct sum.
seo.skills: Decimal Addition practises the column-by-column algorithm with decimal numbers: aligning the decimal point, adding like places (hundredths with hundredths, tenths with tenths), and carrying across columns. These are the same skills as whole-number addition, extended to fractional positions.
seo.example: For 1.47 + 0.86: hundredths 7+6=13, set 3 and carry 1; tenths 4+8+1=13, set 3 and carry 1; ones 1+0+1=2. Answer 2.33. Each step is done on a separate wheel, so no digit is lost.
seo.mistakes: The most common error is ignoring column alignment and adding 1.47 + 0.86 as if they were 147 + 86. Separate wheels for each place prevent that by forcing students to add only the matching column at a time.

faqs.q: How does the column-wheel interface teach decimal-point alignment?
faqs.a: Each wheel is labelled ones, tenths, or hundredths and only accepts its own digit, so students cannot add a tenths digit into the ones column by accident. The structure enforces alignment without a written grid.

faqs.q: Which grades is Decimal Addition for?
faqs.a: Grades 4-5, where students extend column addition to decimals with tenths and hundredths.

faqs.q: Does the game cover subtraction of decimals too?
faqs.a: No. Decimal Addition focuses on the addition algorithm with carrying. Decimal subtraction with borrowing is a separate skill practised in a different activity.

numberBond.title: Number Bond Splitter
numberBond.description: Split a whole number into two parts that compose it, and a crystal bridge spans the chasm
numberBond.instructions: Two stone platforms float over a chasm. Build the bridge: use left −/+ to set the left part and right −/+ to set the right part (you can also drag on each side). The bridge connects when the two parts add up to exactly span the gap. Then press Check
numberBond.correct: Great! The two parts together make {target}
numberBond.wrong: Almost! Make the two parts add up to span the gap exactly, then press Check

seo.intro: Number Bond Splitter presents two floating platforms over a chasm. Students adjust the left and right numbers until they sum to the whole, watching a crystal bridge materialise when the parts are correct. The bridge-over-a-gap metaphor makes part-part-whole relationships tangible at the earliest grade levels.
howToPlay: Read the whole number shown above the chasm.
Use −/+ buttons or drag on each platform to set the left part.
Watch the right part update to show what remains as you adjust the left.
When both parts sum to the whole and the bridge spans the gap, press Check.
seo.skills: Number bonds build automatic decomposition fluency for small numbers (1–20), which is the foundation for mental addition and subtraction. Knowing that 7 splits into 3+4 or 5+2 allows students to add across ten without counting on fingers.
seo.example: For the whole 8, a student sets the left part to 5; the right part automatically shows 3. The bridge appears, confirming 5+3=8. Trying 6 and then 2 shows a different valid split, reinforcing that a number has multiple decompositions.
seo.mistakes: Young students often fix one part at 1 and count up the other, missing that there are many valid pairs. The interactive drag encourages exploring the whole range of splits rather than stopping at the first correct answer.

faqs.q: How does Number Bond Splitter differ from Ten Frame Fill?
faqs.a: Ten Frame Fill shows a number as filled vs. empty cells in a fixed 10-slot grid, building the relationship to ten. Number Bond Splitter is open-ended: it shows any split of any small number into two arbitrary parts, training flexible decomposition independent of ten.

faqs.q: Which grades is Number Bond Splitter for?
faqs.a: Grades 1–2, where students are learning to decompose numbers within 20 and need fluent part-part-whole thinking before moving on to addition algorithms.

faqs.q: Is every split of the whole accepted or only one specific pair?
faqs.a: Any valid pair that sums to the whole is accepted. The game encourages exploring all decompositions of the target number across multiple plays.

numberLineJump.title: Number Line Jump
numberLineJump.description: Hop the car along the race track until you reach the flag
numberLineJump.instructions: Set the jump size with − / +, then press → Forward or ← Back to hop the car along the track. You can hop several times until you land exactly on the flag. Then press Check
numberLineJump.wrong: Almost! Hop forward or back until you land exactly on the flag

seo.intro: Number Line Jump puts a car on a race track number line and asks students to hop it to a target position using jumps of their chosen size. Each forward or backward hop is a visible addition or subtraction, turning the abstract operation into a physical journey along a scaled line.
howToPlay: Read the target number shown by the flag on the track.
Set the jump size using −/+ buttons.
Press Forward or Back to hop the car that many spaces at a time.
Land exactly on the flag, then press Check to finish the round.
seo.skills: Number Line Jump builds flexible mental arithmetic by making addition and subtraction directional: forward for adding, backward for subtracting. Students develop number-line intuition — the sense of distance and direction — which underpins integer operations and later coordinate work.
seo.example: To reach 17 from 0 with jumps of 5: three hops forward land on 15, then switch to a jump of 2 for one more hop to reach 17. Students see 5+5+5+2=17 as a path, not an abstract sum.
seo.mistakes: Students often choose a jump size that does not divide evenly into the target and overshoot, then struggle to backtrack. The game allows mixed jump sizes in one round, encouraging students to plan a two-step approach rather than a single repeated hop.

faqs.q: How does Number Line Jump differ from Skip Count Race?
faqs.a: Skip Count Race fixes the jump size (2s, 5s, or 10s) and asks students to mark every landing, practising regular intervals. Number Line Jump lets the student choose any jump size and make multiple hops of different sizes to reach a single target — it is more open-ended and covers addition and subtraction together.

faqs.q: Which grades is Number Line Jump for?
faqs.a: Grades 1–4, with simpler targets and larger jumps for younger students and two-digit targets requiring mixed jump sizes for older students.

faqs.q: Can the car go backwards to practise subtraction?
faqs.a: Yes. Pressing the Back button hops the car left along the track, modelling subtraction. Students may need to overshoot a target and hop back to reach it exactly.

explodingDots.title: Exploding Dots
explodingDots.description: Add neon dots to the ones box; when a box reaches ten dots they explode into one dot in the box to the left
explodingDots.instructions: Add dots to each box with the − / + buttons (hundreds, tens, ones), or drag up over a box to add. When a box reaches ten dots they explode into one dot in the box to its left. Build the number, then press Check.
explodingDots.wrong: Almost! Check the boxes and fix the number

seo.intro: Exploding Dots turns regrouping into a spectacle. Students place neon dots into ones, tens, and hundreds boxes to represent a target number. When any box reaches ten, those dots explode into one dot in the box to the left — a vivid, rule-free display of why the decimal system works in base ten.
howToPlay: Read the target number displayed above the boxes.
Use the −/+ buttons or drag upward to add dots to the ones, tens, or hundreds box.
When a box reaches ten dots, watch them explode into a single dot moving left.
Adjust until the boxes together represent the target number, then press Check.
seo.skills: Exploding Dots builds deep intuition for base-ten place value. Rather than memorising that ten ones equal one ten, students see it happen as an explosion. This animation-driven understanding of regrouping prepares students for multi-digit addition, subtraction, and eventually number systems in other bases.
seo.example: Building 23: add 3 dots to the ones box (stays as 3), then add 2 dots to the tens box (stays as 2). No explosions needed. Now build 33: add 13 ones — at ten, they explode into one ten, leaving 3 ones. Result: 1 ten from explosion plus 2 tens added = 3 tens, 3 ones = 33.
seo.mistakes: Students often overfill a box and miss the explosion, ending up with more than ten dots in one place. The game freezes boxes that overflow until students trigger the explosion, reinforcing that ten-in-one-box is always the rule.

faqs.q: Which grades does Exploding Dots target?
faqs.a: Grades 2–3, where students are establishing place-value intuition for 2-digit and 3-digit numbers and beginning to understand the logic of carrying in addition.

faqs.q: Is this related to James Tanton's Exploding Dots project?
faqs.a: The mechanic is inspired by the same base-ten dot model popularised in mathematics education. The game uses the same visual language to make regrouping self-evident.

faqs.q: How is this different from Addition Mine?
faqs.a: Exploding Dots focuses purely on representing a single number and seeing the explosion rule. Addition Mine uses the same columns but in the context of adding two numbers together with the mine-cart carrying mechanic.

factris.title: Factris
factris.description: Reshape a stack of warehouse blocks so it exactly fills the glowing storage shelf
factris.instructions: Change the stack's width with the − / + buttons (or drag right to widen, left to narrow) until the blocks form a full rectangle that exactly fills the glowing shelf. If the width doesn't fit, the top row stays incomplete — that's not a clean rectangle. Then press Check.
factris.prompt: Arrange {area} blocks to fill the shelf
factris.wrong: Almost! Change the width until the blocks exactly fill the glowing shelf

seo.intro: Factris gives multiplication and factoring a Tetris-style twist. A fixed count of warehouse blocks must be arranged into a perfect rectangle that exactly fills a glowing shelf. Students find a working width — meaning a factor of the total — and the height fills in automatically, making the link between area, arrays, and multiplication facts vivid.
howToPlay: Read the total number of blocks shown in the prompt.
Use −/+ buttons or drag left/right to change the width of the stack.
Watch the height adjust so that width × height always equals the total.
Find a width that fills every cell in the glowing shelf with no gaps or overhang, then press Check.
seo.skills: Factris builds fluency with factors, multiples, and the relationship between multiplication and area. Searching for a width that works is identical to finding a factor pair, so students internalise factor pairs through spatial trial and error rather than rote memorisation.
seo.example: The shelf holds 12 blocks. Width 3 gives height 4 — the rectangle fills perfectly. Width 5 leaves 2 orphan blocks in the top row — not a factor. Students quickly learn that 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12 all work; 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11 do not.
seo.mistakes: Students often try every number linearly instead of reasoning about factors. Noticing that width × height = total and using that to guess smarter widths is the insight the game is designed to surface.

faqs.q: Which grades is Factris designed for?
faqs.a: Grades 3–5, covering factor pairs, multiples, and area models of multiplication — core content in upper primary arithmetic.

faqs.q: How is this different from Multiplication Array?
faqs.a: Multiplication Array shows a pre-built grid of objects and asks students to count rows × columns. Factris gives a total and asks students to find a valid factor pair by reshaping the stack — it adds the search for factors to the array model.

faqs.q: Does the game show all factor pairs or just one?
faqs.a: The game accepts any valid width. Students who try multiple widths naturally discover all factor pairs of the total, turning the session into an implicit factor-pair audit.

skipCount.title: Skip Count Race
skipCount.description: Mark every skip along the safari track, then watch the animal dash across them
skipCount.instructions: Tap the tiles the animal lands on at each skip. Mark them all the way to the end, then press Check — the animal will hop across the stones. Tap a tile again to unmark it, or Reset to start over.
skipCount.correct: Great! You skip-counted by {step} up to {target}
skipCount.wrong: Almost! Mark exactly every skip — none missing and none extra

seo.intro: Skip Count Race sends a safari animal across stepping stones on a track. Students tap each stone the animal lands on when counting by 2s, 5s, or 10s, and the animal dashes across to confirm the sequence. Marking the pattern themselves before seeing the animation makes the regularity of skip counting active and memorable.
howToPlay: Read the skip interval and target shown above the safari track.
Tap each stone that falls on a multiple of the skip interval.
Work all the way to the target, marking every landing spot and no others.
Press Check to watch the animal hop across your marked stones.
seo.skills: Skip counting by 2s, 5s, and 10s is the on-ramp to multiplication tables. Identifying every multiple of a given number trains rhythmic counting and lays the conceptual groundwork for understanding multiplication as repeated addition along a structured sequence.
seo.example: Skip-counting by 5s to 30: tap tiles 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. The even spacing of tapped stones reveals the 5× pattern visually before any times-table is introduced.
seo.mistakes: Students often miss the first skip (starting at 0 instead of the interval) or mark the target tile but forget the ones before it. The game requires every multiple to be tapped — a missing or extra stone fails the check — reinforcing the complete sequence, not just the final answer.

faqs.q: How does Skip Count Race differ from Number Line Jump?
faqs.a: Skip Count Race fixes the jump size and asks students to mark every landing point in a sequence — practising multiples as a pattern. Number Line Jump is goal-directed: students choose any jump size and hop to a single target, practising flexible addition and subtraction.

faqs.q: Which grades is Skip Count Race for?
faqs.a: Grades 2–3, where students consolidate 2s, 5s, and 10s skip counting as direct preparation for multiplication.

faqs.q: Does the game cover skip counts other than 2, 5, and 10?
faqs.a: The core game uses 2s, 5s, and 10s, which align with the national curriculum for grades 2–3. More intervals may appear in advanced rounds as students progress.

weightBalance.title: Weighing Scale
weightBalance.description: Balance the fruit using gram weights at a market stall
weightBalance.instructions: Add gram weights to the right pan with the − / + buttons (1 g, 10 g, 100 g), or drag up to add a 10 g weight, until the beam is level. Then press Check
weightBalance.wrong: Almost! The beam is still tilted — check the total weight and fix it

seo.intro: Weighing Scale puts students at a market stall where they must balance fruit on one pan by adding gram weights (1 g, 10 g, 100 g) to the other. The tilting beam provides instant feedback on whether the total is too light or too heavy, turning weight measurement into a concrete physical balancing task.
howToPlay: Read the target weight shown for the fruit in the left pan.
Use the 100 g, 10 g, and 1 g buttons to add weights to the right pan.
Watch the beam: tilted left means the right side is too light; tilted right means too heavy.
Press Check once the beam is perfectly level.
seo.skills: Composing a target weight from 1 g, 10 g, and 100 g denominations builds place-value intuition for three-digit numbers while practising the real measurement skill of choosing the most efficient combination of weights — the same reasoning used in every market and kitchen context for grades 2–3.
seo.example: The target is 253 g. Students add two 100 g weights (200 g), five 10 g weights (50 g), and three 1 g weights (3 g) to reach exactly 253 g. The step-by-step decomposition mirrors the place-value structure of the number itself.
seo.mistakes: Students often use too many small weights instead of exchanging ten 1 g pieces for a 10 g piece. The limited number of weights on screen encourages the regrouping step — paralleling the carrying concept in written addition.

faqs.q: How does choosing gram weights reinforce place value in this game?
faqs.a: The three denominations (1 g, 10 g, 100 g) map directly to ones, tens, and hundreds. Building a weight like 253 g makes the three-digit structure physical, not abstract.

faqs.q: Which grades is Weighing Scale designed for?
faqs.a: Grades 2–3, where students learn standard mass units (grams), practise three-digit composition, and begin using measurement instruments.

faqs.q: How is this different from the Balance Scale game (arithmetic)?
faqs.a: Weighing Scale (units topic) measures physical mass in grams and builds measurement and place-value skills. Balance Scale (arithmetic topic) practises mental addition by matching a sum with numbered weights — it is about equality and arithmetic, not mass units.

percentBar.title: Percent Bar
percentBar.description: Fill the stadium stand to the right percent out of 100%
percentBar.instructions: Use the percent ±5% buttons, or drag up to add fans, to fill the stand to the right percent, then press Check
percentBar.correct: Great! You filled {percent}% of the stand
percentBar.wrong: Almost! Check the percent and fix the fill

seo.intro: Percent Bar shows a stadium stand divided into 100 equal sections. Students drag or tap to fill exactly the right number of sections, making the abstract idea that percent means parts out of 100 concrete and immediate. Unlike a written fraction, the bar is always the same total length.
howToPlay: Read the target percentage shown above the stand.
Use the +5% and -5% buttons, or drag upward, to fill fans into the stand.
Watch the fill level and the live percentage counter update together.
When the filled portion matches the target, press Check.
seo.skills: Percent Bar anchors the core definition: percent is always out of 100. Filling the same fixed bar to different levels builds the spatial sense that 50% is half, 25% is a quarter, and 75% is three-quarters, before any formula is introduced.
seo.example: For 30% the student fills 30 of the 100 sections. Comparing the result to a half-full bar makes it obvious that 30% is less than half, without any calculation.
seo.mistakes: Students sometimes confuse the percent value with the number of fans to add rather than the total fill level. The fixed 100-section bar corrects this: reaching 30% always means exactly 30 sections filled, regardless of how many clicks it took.

faqs.q: How does filling a bar help students understand percent as parts out of 100?
faqs.a: The bar is always 100 sections wide, so filling it to 40% literally places 40 out of 100 sections. The visual length makes the fraction-out-of-100 definition self-evident before any rule is stated.

faqs.q: Which grades is Percent Bar for?
faqs.a: Grades 5-6, where students first define percent as a ratio out of 100 and need a visual model before working with percent calculations.

faqs.q: How is Percent Bar different from Percent of a Number?
faqs.a: Percent Bar is a visual model: students shade a fixed bar to see what a percent looks like. Percent of a Number is a calculation game: students compute a specific quantity such as 30% of 80. One builds the concept; the other applies it.

percentOfQuantity.title: Percent of a Number
percentOfQuantity.description: Calculate what a percentage of a quantity is — mental math practice with percentages
percentOfQuantity.instructions: Read the problem on the sign, set your answer with the − / + buttons, then press Check

seo.intro: Percent of a Number presents a sign showing a percentage and a quantity, and students set the numeric answer using buttons. The game trains the two-step mental calculation: convert the percent to a decimal or fraction, then multiply by the whole, making percentage arithmetic fluent and fast.
howToPlay: Read the percentage and the quantity shown on the sign.
Calculate mentally: convert the percent to a fraction (e.g. 25% = 1/4) or multiply by the decimal form.
Use the +/- buttons to set your calculated answer.
Press Check to confirm.
seo.skills: Percent of a Number builds automatic fluency with the percent-of calculation: finding 10%, 25%, 50%, and other friendly percents of a whole number. This is the computational skill that underlies discounts, tips, tax, and every real-world percentage situation.
seo.example: For 25% of 80: students recognise 25% as one-quarter, then divide 80 by 4 to get 20. Practicing friendly percents first makes trickier values like 15% or 35% approachable by decomposition.
seo.mistakes: A common error is computing the percent value directly (writing 25 instead of 20 for 25% of 80). The game exposes this by requiring the actual quantity, not the percent symbol, training students to complete both steps of the calculation.

faqs.q: What two-step calculation does this game practise?
faqs.a: Students convert the percent to a fraction or decimal, then multiply by the given quantity. Both steps must be done correctly to reach the right answer, building fluency with the full calculation.

faqs.q: Which grades is Percent of a Number for?
faqs.a: Grades 5-6, where students move from understanding what a percent represents to calculating a percent of a given quantity in real-world contexts.

faqs.q: How is this different from Percent Discount?
faqs.a: Percent of a Number asks for the raw percent-of result (what is 30% of 80?). Percent Discount applies that calculation to a shopping context: find the discount amount, then subtract it from the original price to get the sale price.

percentDiscount.title: Shop Discount
percentDiscount.description: Calculate the sale price after a discount — real-world percentage practice
percentDiscount.instructions: Read the original price and discount percentage, set the sale price with the − / + buttons, then press Check
percentDiscount.prompt: An item costs ₪{original} with a {discount}% discount. What is the sale price?
percentDiscount.wrong: Not quite — calculate the discount amount then subtract from the original price

seo.intro: Shop Discount sets students in a virtual store where they must calculate the final sale price after a percentage discount. By working through a realistic shopping transaction, students practise the two-stage process: compute the discount amount, then subtract it from the original price.
howToPlay: Read the original price and the discount percentage shown in the shop.
Calculate the discount amount: apply the percentage to the original price.
Subtract the discount from the original price to find the sale price.
Use the +/- buttons to enter the sale price, then press Check.
seo.skills: Shop Discount develops the real-world application of percent-of: computing a percentage reduction and applying it. Students learn that finding a discount is a two-step process (percent of a whole, then subtraction), which is the foundation for calculating tax, tips, and commission.
seo.example: An item costs 200 with a 25% discount. Students calculate 25% of 200 = 50, then subtract: 200 - 50 = 150. The sale price is 150. Seeing the full workflow in a shopping frame makes each step purposeful.
seo.mistakes: Many students enter the discount amount itself (50) rather than the final sale price (150). The game labels the input field as sale price, directing students to complete the subtraction step that distinguishes this game from a plain percent-of calculation.

faqs.q: Why does this game ask for the sale price rather than the discount amount?
faqs.a: Computing the full sale price requires both steps: finding the percent-of and subtracting. Stopping at the discount amount skips the subtraction stage that is needed in every real discount situation.

faqs.q: Which grades is Shop Discount for?
faqs.a: Grades 5-6, where students apply percent calculations to real-world contexts including shopping, tax, and other percentage-based adjustments.

faqs.q: How is Shop Discount different from Percent Bar?
faqs.a: Percent Bar is a visual model that shows what a percentage looks like on a fixed bar of 100 sections. Shop Discount is a two-step calculation problem set in a real context, requiring students to compute a percent-of and then apply it to find a new price.

ratioMixer.title: Ratio Mixer
ratioMixer.description: Mix two juices at the smoothie bar to match the target ratio — and discover that equivalent ratios taste the same.
ratioMixer.instructions: Set the amount of Juice A and Juice B with the − and + buttons (or drag a glass up to fill it, down to empty it) until their ratio matches the target — any equivalent amounts work — then press Check.
ratioMixer.wrong: Not the right ratio yet — adjust the amounts and try again

seo.intro: Ratio Mixer places students at a smoothie bar with two juice glasses. The goal is to pour Juice A and Juice B in a target ratio — and discover that 1:2, 2:4, and 3:6 all produce the same drink. The act of adjusting amounts until the ratio clicks makes equivalence feel concrete rather than symbolic.
howToPlay: Read the target ratio shown above the glasses (for example, 1:2).
Use the − and + buttons, or drag each glass up or down, to set the amounts of Juice A and Juice B.
Any pair of amounts that forms an equivalent ratio will be accepted — experiment freely.
Press Check when your mix matches the target ratio.
seo.skills: Ratio Mixer builds the core understanding that a ratio describes a relationship between two quantities, not a fixed pair of numbers. Students discover that multiplying both parts by the same factor preserves the ratio — the foundation for proportional reasoning in grades 5 and 6.
seo.example: For a target of 1:2, pouring 3 units of Juice A and 6 units of Juice B is accepted because 3:6 simplifies to 1:2. Students see that the flavour — the ratio — stays the same regardless of total volume.
seo.mistakes: Students often fix one amount and only adjust the other, which rarely keeps the ratio correct. The game teaches that both quantities must scale together, correcting the common belief that only one side of a ratio can change.

faqs.q: How does adjusting amounts in Ratio Mixer differ from filling a ratio table in the Ratio Table game?
faqs.a: Ratio Mixer asks students to find any valid combination by experimenting with two quantities simultaneously. Ratio Table presents a structured grid where students compute a single missing value using a fixed scaling factor.

faqs.q: Which grades is Ratio Mixer for?
faqs.a: Grades 5-6, where students first define ratio as a relationship between two quantities and explore equivalent ratios as part of the proportional reasoning strand.

faqs.q: Why does the game accept any equivalent ratio, not just the target numbers?
faqs.a: Accepting all equivalent forms is the lesson: 2:4 and 3:6 are the same ratio as 1:2. If only one answer were correct, students would guess numbers rather than understand why the ratio is preserved.

ratioRecipe.title: Recipe Scaling
ratioRecipe.description: Scale a recipe to a different number of servings — practice multiplying quantities by a ratio
ratioRecipe.instructions: Read the original recipe, set the required ingredient amount with the − / + buttons, then press Check
ratioRecipe.prompt: Scale the recipe to {servings} servings. How much {ingredient} is needed?
ratioRecipe.wrong: Almost! Multiply the original amount by the serving ratio

seo.intro: Recipe Scaling shows a kitchen recipe card for a fixed number of servings and asks students to recompute every ingredient for a different serving count. Scaling a recipe by a whole-number factor — say, tripling flour from 200g to 600g — gives ratio multiplication a real-world purpose that abstract drill cannot match.
howToPlay: Read the original recipe: note the number of servings and each ingredient amount.
Check the target serving count shown above the recipe card.
Calculate the scaling factor: new servings divided by original servings.
Multiply the displayed ingredient by that factor and enter your answer with the − / + buttons.
Press Check to confirm.
seo.skills: Recipe Scaling practises multiplicative scaling: identifying the ratio between two serving counts and applying it to each ingredient. This is the applied core of proportional reasoning — the same skill used in unit conversion, map reading, and percentage problems in grades 5 and 6.
seo.example: A recipe for 4 servings needs 200 g of flour. Scaling to 12 servings requires a factor of 3 (12 ÷ 4), so flour becomes 200 × 3 = 600 g. Students must identify the factor first, then apply it — two distinct steps.
seo.mistakes: Students often add the serving difference rather than multiply by the factor (e.g. adding 8 to 200 g instead of multiplying by 3). The recipe context makes the error obvious: adding makes the recipe only slightly bigger rather than truly tripled.

faqs.q: What does scaling a recipe teach that the mixing mechanic in Ratio Mixer does not?
faqs.a: Recipe Scaling makes the scaling factor explicit: students must divide the new serving count by the original to find it, then multiply each ingredient. Ratio Mixer focuses on recognising equivalent ratios without computing a named factor.

faqs.q: Which grades is Recipe Scaling for?
faqs.a: Grades 5-6, where students apply proportional reasoning to multi-step real-world problems and practise multiplying by a ratio expressed as a whole number or simple fraction.

faqs.q: Does the game ask about one ingredient or all of them?
faqs.a: Each round focuses on one named ingredient chosen from the recipe. This keeps the calculation to one step per round while still requiring students to identify the correct scaling factor from the serving counts.

ratioTable.title: Ratio Table
ratioTable.description: Fill in the missing value in a ratio table — practice scaling ratios
ratioTable.instructions: Read the table, set the missing value with the − / + buttons, then press Check
ratioTable.prompt: In the ratio table {a}:{b}, fill in the missing value
ratioTable.wrong: Almost! Calculate the value using the scaling factor

seo.intro: Ratio Table presents a grid of equivalent ratios with one value missing. Students read across the rows to find the scaling factor that connects column to column, then fill in the blank. The table format makes the pattern of equivalent ratios visible as a structure, not just isolated pairs.
howToPlay: Read the ratio table: each column shows a pair of values in the same ratio.
Compare two complete columns to identify the scaling factor between them.
Apply that factor to the known value in the incomplete column to find the missing value.
Enter the missing value with the − / + buttons, then press Check.
seo.skills: Working with a ratio table builds the habit of reading relationships across rows, not just within a single pair. This row-based thinking is the direct preparation for setting up and solving proportions, unit rate problems, and function tables in grades 4 through 6.
seo.example: In a 3:5 ratio table, the first column shows 3 and 5, the second shows 6 and 10, and the third shows 9 and a blank. The scaling factor from column 1 to column 3 is 3, so the missing value is 5 × 3 = 15.
seo.mistakes: Students sometimes add a constant instead of multiplying (for example, adding 3 to each value across rows). The table layout makes this error visible because the pattern breaks in the other row — the structured grid is its own correction tool.

faqs.q: How does the table layout help students see the pattern in equivalent ratios?
faqs.a: Aligning multiple equivalent pairs in columns lets students spot the scaling factor visually: both rows grow by the same multiplier across columns. This structure is harder to see when ratios are presented as isolated pairs.

faqs.q: Which grades is Ratio Table for?
faqs.a: Grades 4-6: grade 4 students use simpler whole-number tables to explore equivalent ratios, while grades 5-6 work with larger factors as part of formal proportional reasoning.

faqs.q: How is Ratio Table different from Ratio Mixer and Recipe Scaling?
faqs.a: Ratio Table focuses on reading and extending a structured grid of equivalent pairs, training pattern recognition across multiple ratios at once. Ratio Mixer asks students to find any valid equivalent by adjusting two quantities freely; Recipe Scaling asks them to apply a single computed factor to a real-world ingredient.

numberSequence.title: Number Sequence
numberSequence.description: Find the rule and fill in the missing stone in the sequence
numberSequence.instructions: Find the rule of the sequence (add or multiply), set the missing stone with the − / + buttons (or drag up to raise it), then press Check
numberSequence.wrong: Almost! Find the rule and try again

seo.intro: Number Sequence places a row of numbered stones with one stone missing and asks students to figure out the rule — add the same amount each time, or multiply — then supply the missing value. It bridges visual pattern work toward rule-based arithmetic thinking for grades 3–5.
howToPlay: Read all the visible numbers in the row and look for what changes between each pair.
Decide whether the rule is additive (each step adds the same amount) or multiplicative (each step multiplies).
Use the − / + buttons, or drag the missing stone upward, to set the correct value.
Press Check to confirm your answer.
seo.skills: Finding a rule from a sequence of numbers and applying it trains skip-counting, arithmetic-progression recognition, and early function thinking — skills that feed directly into multiplication tables, input-output tables, and pattern algebra in grades 3–5.
seo.example: A sequence shows 3, 6, 9, ?, 15. Each step adds 3, so the missing number is 12. Seeing that the gap between each pair is equal — not just reading the last number — is the reasoning skill Number Sequence develops.
seo.mistakes: Students commonly guess by adding one more than the previous term instead of measuring the actual gap. Number Sequence requires checking multiple gaps to confirm the rule, training the habit of verifying before answering.

faqs.q: How does the missing-stone mechanic teach rule-finding rather than just answer-guessing?
faqs.a: The stone appears anywhere in the sequence — not always at the end — so students must establish the rule from the surrounding terms rather than extending a list by one more step.

faqs.q: Which grades is Number Sequence for?
faqs.a: It suits grades 3–5, where students move from visual patterns to numeric rules and begin formalising arithmetic and multiplicative relationships.

faqs.q: How is Number Sequence different from Geometric Sequence?
faqs.a: Number Sequence mixes additive and simple multiplicative rules; Geometric Sequence focuses exclusively on constant-ratio multiplication such as ×2 or ×3 — the next level of difficulty.

geometricSequence.title: Geometric Sequence
geometricSequence.description: Find the multiplication rule and fill in the missing stone — multiplication sequences
geometricSequence.instructions: Find the rule (×2 or ×3), set the missing stone with the − / + buttons, then press Check
geometricSequence.wrong: Almost! Look for the multiplier between consecutive terms

seo.intro: Geometric Sequence gives students a row of numbered stones where every term is obtained by multiplying the previous one by a fixed ratio — ×2, ×3, or similar. Identifying that constant multiplier and using it to fill the missing stone is the defining skill of this grade 4–6 game.
howToPlay: Read the visible numbers and divide any term by the one before it to find the common ratio.
Confirm the ratio holds for every adjacent pair — it must be the same throughout.
Use the − / + buttons (or drag the stone) to set the missing value.
Press Check to see whether the ratio is consistent across the whole sequence.
seo.skills: Recognising and applying a constant multiplicative ratio is the foundation of geometric sequences, exponential growth patterns, and percentage calculations. Geometric Sequence builds this fluency before students meet the formal algebra of exponents in grades 4–6.
seo.example: A sequence shows 2, 6, 18, ?, 162. Each term is multiplied by 3, so the missing value is 54. The key insight is that dividing any two consecutive terms (18 ÷ 6 = 3) reveals the rule — this game trains that division-as-ratio move.
seo.mistakes: Students often try to add a constant difference rather than finding the multiplier, because additive thinking is easier. Geometric Sequence makes the multiplier the only thing that works, forcing students to switch from additive to multiplicative reasoning.

faqs.q: Why does the game use division to find the rule rather than just reading a ×2 or ×3 label?
faqs.a: Deriving the ratio by dividing consecutive terms — rather than being told it — trains the algebraic habit of extracting a rule from data. That reasoning transfers directly to tables, graphs, and later exponential work.

faqs.q: Which grades is Geometric Sequence for?
faqs.a: It targets grades 4–6, where students are fluent in multiplication and ready to see that the same operation applied repeatedly creates exponential growth.

faqs.q: How is Geometric Sequence different from Number Sequence?
faqs.a: Geometric Sequence uses only multiplicative rules (constant ratio); Number Sequence includes additive patterns too. Geometric Sequence is the harder, more focused version of the same idea.

fractionStrip.title: Fraction Strips
fractionStrip.description: Build a fraction equal to the given one using two wooden strips and a balance
fractionStrip.instructions: The top strip is given. Build the bottom one: set the number of saw-cuts with denominator −/+ and the colored pieces with numerator −/+ (or drag: right = more colored pieces, up = more cuts), so it is the same SIZE as the top strip but with a different number of pieces. The balance levels when the two fractions are equal. Then press Check
fractionStrip.wrong: Almost! Build a strip the same size but with a different denominator — the balance will level
